original_abstract: The accumulation of high nitrate concentrations in global water resources poses a severe threat to both environmental stability and human health, necessitating the development of robust remediation technologies. Single-atom alloys (SAAs) have recently emerged as a transformative bimetallic architecture for various catalytic applications, including the nitrate reduction reaction (NRR). In this work, we identify a profound divergence between thermocatalytic (T-NRR) and electrocatalytic (E-NRR) pathways, leading to starkly different performance profiles for SAA catalysts. Leveraging a series of Pd/Cu nanoalloys with Pd:Cu ratios ranging from 1:100 to 100:1, we find that the Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> SAA achieves exceptional performance in E-NRR, characterized by superior activity (TOF<sub>Pd</sub> = 2 min<sup>-1</sup>) and high N<sub>2</sub> selectivity (94%). Conversely, this same SAA configuration performs poorly under thermocatalytic conditions compared to its more Pd-rich nanoalloy counterparts. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations reveal that the enhanced E-NRR performance of Pd/Cu<sub>(1:100)</sub> stems from the increased stability of NO<sub>3</sub>* intermediates and a lower kinetic barrier for N<sub>2</sub> formation relative to NH. These advantages are driven by localized pH shifts and the facilitated extraction of protons from water molecules at the electrochemical interface. This study elucidates the critical mechanistic distinctions between T-NRR and E-NRR, providing a framework for the rational design of SAA catalysts tailored for specific catalytic environments.
humanized_abstract: The ever-increasing levels of nitrate in water bodies around the globe pose a major challenge to sustainable development and human health. Thus, there is an urgent need to develop efficient methods to mitigate nitrate pollution. Single-atom alloys (SAAs) have recently introduced a new type of bimetallic structure that is applied in various catalytic reactions, such as nitrate reduction reaction (NRR). Herein, by employing a series of Pd/Cu nanoalloys with varying Pd to Cu atomic ratio in the range from 1:100 to 100:1, we found that Pd/Cu (1:100) SAA presents the highest performance for the E-NRR, with a turnover frequency (TOF) of 2 min(-1) and a nitrogen selectivity of 94%. Interestingly, under the same condition, the Pd/Cu (1:100) SAA shows lower catalytic activity and nitride selectivity for T-NRR than that of PdCu (5:2) and PdCu (2:5) nanoalloys. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the higher catalytic performance of Pd/Cu (1:100) for E-NRR can be attributed to the more stable NO3* adsorption species and lower energy barrier for N2 formation compared with that of NH. The advantages for E-NRR on Pd/Cu (1:100) SAA are attributed to the pH value of the surrounding electrolyte environment and the amount of protons removed from the water molecules on the surface of catalyst.
